text.title: Performance Test Types
text.summary: Five test types — load, stress, spike, endurance, scalability — each with a distinct purpose, duration, and user profile.

rule.statement: Use load tests to verify system behavior under expected traffic; run for an extended period (hours) at normal-to-peak users.
rule.rationale: Load tests establish the production-shaped baseline and confirm SLOs hold under representative concurrency.

rule.statement: Use stress tests to find the breaking point; run until failure with users exceeding maximum expected capacity.
rule.rationale: Knowing where the system breaks informs autoscaling thresholds and circuit-breaker settings.

rule.statement: Use spike tests to validate behavior under sudden load jumps (low to high in seconds); run in short bursts.
rule.rationale: Marketing campaigns and viral events produce spike shapes, not gradual ramps.

rule.statement: Use endurance (soak) tests at normal load for 12-72 hours to detect memory leaks and resource exhaustion.
rule.rationale: Memory leaks and file-descriptor leaks are invisible in short runs; only sustained load makes the trend measurable.

rule.statement: Use scalability tests with incrementally increasing users to verify that horizontal/vertical scaling produces proportional throughput gains.
rule.rationale: A system that does not scale linearly has a bottleneck that must be found before capacity planning is trustworthy.

rule.statement: Record p50, p95, p99, error rate, RPS, concurrent users, and duration for every load-test run. A single average latency number lies.
rule.rationale: Tail latency (p99) is where users churn; aggregate averages hide bimodal distributions caused by slow endpoints or N+1 queries.

rule.statement: Never load-test from the same machine as the system under test — observer effect dominates the results.
rule.rationale: CPU and network contention on the test machine competes with the target, producing artificially low throughput numbers.

rule.statement: Warm caches and connection pools before measuring (one full pass at low concurrency) so JIT/connection-pool effects do not dominate.
rule.rationale: Cold-start numbers are not representative of sustained production behavior and will produce misleading baselines.

rule.statement: Store raw results (not summaries) so you can re-aggregate later with different percentile cuts.
rule.rationale: Summarized results cannot be re-analyzed; raw results survive SLO changes and retrospective root-cause work.

rule.statement: Run all load tests against a staging environment that mirrors production in hardware, data volume, and network topology.
rule.rationale: Perf numbers from a weak-hardware or empty-database environment are not transferable to production.

rule.statement: Hard-code an environment allowlist in every load-test script (e.g., assert "prod" not in TARGET_URL) to prevent accidental production load.
rule.rationale: Agents running automated load tests will target production by accident without this guard.

rule.statement: For Locust, enforce a gradual spawn-rate profile (e.g., 10 users/second) rather than setting spawn_rate = total_users.
rule.rationale: Spawning all users simultaneously overwhelms the target before the test starts, invalidating the measurement.
